coredata.dc:title: A novel DNA based bioassay toward ultrasensitive detection of Brucella using gold nanoparticles supported histidine: A new platform for the assay of bacteria in the cultured and human biofluids with and without polymerase chain reactions (PCR)
coredata.dc:creator: Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Babaie, Parinaz
Mokhtarzadeh, Ahad
Hajizadeh, Nader
Mahboob, Soltanali
coredata.dc:description: Brucella organisms, which are small aerobic intracellular coccobacilli, localize in the reproductive organs of host animals, causing abortions and sterility. In this work, we used a novel method to preparation of excellent genosensor on the surface of low-toxic substrate (gold nanoparticles) supported histidine prepared by fully electrodeposition method. The results of the present work show that the nano-Au-Hist provide suitable active sites for the DNA probe immobilization. The fabricated DNA genosensor employs cyclic voltammetry (CV), and square wave voltammetry (SWV) techniques for monitoring the behavior of the redox probe. To survey the morphological pattern and surface structural characterizations, the Field Emission-Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) has been applied. In summary, the gold nanoparticles supported by histidine was checked for immobilization of a Brucella-specific probe and detection of hybridization with a variety of sequences with a high sensitivity. The high sensitivity would be related to more favorable conformation and deflection angle of the probe for an efficient hybridization, higher surface concentration of the probe, and/or enhanced diffusion regime. These lead to better display of the entangled target sequences arising from the nanobiotechnology. The proposed genosensor showed a perfect distinction between complementary, non-complementary and mismatched DNA sequences. The engineered genosensor for detection of the complementary/non-complementary sequences were assayed. The fabricated genosensor was evaluated for the assay of the bacteria in the cultured and human samples with and without polymerase chain reactions (PCR). The genosensor could detect the complementary sequence in linear concentration range of 1 × 10−1 to 1 × 10−10 μM, and a low limit of quantification 0.1 pM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticle
Brucellosis
DNA
Genosensing
Hybridization
Biomedical analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the Au electrode modified by gold nanoparticles supported by histidine (nan-Au-Hist)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chronoamperograms of Au electrode in the solution containinig 0.01 M HAuCl4 + 0.1 M H2SO4 + 0.1 M histidine at E = 0 V for 300 s.
FE-SEM image of Au/nano Au-Hist in different magnification.
EDS and elementary analysis table of Au/nano-Au-Hist.

(A&B) CVs and SWVs of the Au/nano Au-Hist in different cycle numer (n = 1, 3, 6, and 11). Scan rate is 100 mV/s. Supporting electrolyte is 0.01 M (Fe (CN)6 3/4-KCl). (C&D) Histogram of peak current variation versus number of cycle.

A) CVs of Au/nano Au-Hist in 0.01 M (Fe (CN)6 3/4-KCl) in various potential sweep rates (from inner to outer): 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 90, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 50, 600, 700, 800, 900, and 1000 mV s−1, respectively. B) Dependence of anodic/cathodic peak currents vs. potential sweep rate. C) Dependence of anodic/cathodic peak currents vs. square root of potential sweep rate. D) Dependence of the peak potential on Ln ν. E) Tafel plot (variation of peak potential versus Ln Ipa. F) Dependency of Ln Ipa versus Ln v.

SWVs of the step-by-step preparation of genosensor. Scan rate is 100 mV/s. Supporting electrolyte is 0.01 M (Fe (CN)6 3/4-KCl). MB = 20 μM. pH 7.4. (Concentration of ss-DNA = 49.8 μM p-ssDNA. Incubation time is 4 h in −4 0C). Concentration of MCH = 0.01 M. Incubation time = 30 min.

SWVs of MB interaction with target and revers sequence of probe. Scan rate is 100 mV/s. Supporting electrolyte is 0.01 M (Fe (CN)6 3/4-KCl). MB = 20 μM. pH 7.4. (Concentration of ss-DNA = 49.8 μM p-ssDNA. Incubation time is 4 h in −4 °C). Concentration of MCH = 0.01 M. Incubation time = 30 min.

SWVs for the regeneration of the genosensor before/after hybridization and after re-hybridization. Scan rate is 100 mV/s. Supporting electrolyte is 0.01 M (Fe (CN)6 3/4-KCl). MB = 20 μM. pH 7.4. (Concentration of ss-DNA = 49.8 μM p-ssDNA. Incubation time is 4 h in −4 °C). Concentration of MCH = 0.01 M. Incubation time = 30 min.

SWVs of the engineered genosensor in different concentrations of Brucella (50, 10, 1, 0.1, 0.01, 0.001, 0.001, 0.00001, 0.000001, 0.0000001, 0.00000001, 0.000000001, 0.0000000001, and 0.00000000001 μM). Inset: calibration curve of the genosensor. Scan rate is 100 mV/s. Supporting electrolyte is 0.01 M (Fe (CN)6 3/4-KCl). MB = 20 μM. pH 7.4. (Concentration of ss-DNA = 49.8 μM p-ssDNA. Incubation time is 4 h in −4 °C). Concentration of MCH = 0.01 M. Incubation time = 30 min.

A) SWVs of the engineered genosensor in different days of storage (1–7 days). B): Histograms of the electrode stability. Scan rate is 100 mV/s. Supporting electrolyte is 0.01 M (Fe (CN)6 3/4-KCl). MB = 20 μM. pH 7.4. (Concentration of ss-DNA = 49.8 μM p-ssDNA. Incubation time is 4 h in −4 °C). Concentration of MCH = 0.01 M. Incubation time = 30 min.

SWVs of the MB recorded using the genosensor at of bacterial genomes of Mycobacterium tuberculosis, E. coli, Bacillus anthracis. Scan rate is 100 mV/s. Supporting electrolyte is 0.01 M (Fe (CN)6 3/4-KCl). MB = 20 μM. pH 7.4. (Concentration of ss-DNA = 49.8 μM p-ssDNA. Incubation time is 4 h in −4 °C). Concentration of MCH = 0.01 M. Incubation time = 30 min.

SWVs for MB recorded using the genosensor for the positive and negative control samples. Scan rate is 100 mV/s. Supporting electrolyte is 0.01 M (Fe (CN)6 3/4-KCl). MB = 20 μM. pH 7.4. (Concentration of ss-DNA = 49.8 μM p-ssDNA. Incubation time is 4 h in −4 °C). Concentration of MCH = 0.01 M. Incubation time = 30 min.

head.ce:title: A novel DNA based bioassay toward ultrasensitive detection of Brucella using gold nanoparticles supported histidine: A new platform for the assay of bacteria in the cultured and human biofluids with and without polymerase chain reactions (PCR)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Higher Education Institute of Rab-Rashid, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Immunology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Brucella organisms, which are small aerobic intracellular coccobacilli, localize in the reproductive organs of host animals, causing abortions and sterility. In this work, we used a novel method to preparation of excellent genosensor on the surface of low-toxic substrate (gold nanoparticles) supported histidine prepared by fully electrodeposition method. The results of the present work show that the nano-Au-Hist provide suitable active sites for the DNA probe immobilization. The fabricated DNA genosensor employs cyclic voltammetry (CV), and square wave voltammetry (SWV) techniques for monitoring the behavior of the redox probe. To survey the morphological pattern and surface structural characterizations, the Field Emission-Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) has been applied. In summary, the gold nanoparticles supported by histidine was checked for immobilization of a Brucella-specific probe and detection of hybridization with a variety of sequences with a high sensitivity. The high sensitivity would be related to more favorable conformation and deflection angle of the probe for an efficient hybridization, higher surface concentration of the probe, and/or enhanced diffusion regime. These lead to better display of the entangled target sequences arising from the nanobiotechnology. The proposed genosensor showed a perfect distinction between complementary, non-complementary and mismatched DNA sequences. The engineered genosensor for detection of the complementary/non-complementary sequences were assayed. The fabricated genosensor was evaluated for the assay of the bacteria in the cultured and human samples with and without polymerase chain reactions (PCR). The genosensor could detect the complementary sequence in linear concentration range of 1 × 10−1 to 1 × 10−10 μM, and a low limit of quantification 0.1 pM.

ce:section.ce:para: Brucellosis is a zoonotic infection caused by the bacterial genus Brucella. The bacteria are transmitted from animals to humans by ingestion through infected food products, direct contact with an infected animal, or inhalation of aerosols. The disease is an old one that has been known by various names, including Mediterranean fever, Malta fever, gastric remittent fever, and undulant fever. Humans are accidental hosts, but brucellosis continues to be a major public health concern worldwide and is the most common zoonotic infection [1].

Brucella organisms, which are small aerobic intracellular coccobacilli, localize in the reproductive organs of host animals, causing abortions and sterility. They are shed in large numbers in the animal's urine, milk, placental fluid, and other fluids.

Nodaway, different methods such as polymerase chain reactions (PCR) and so on have been used for the detection of Brucella [1]. But none of these methods are quantitative which do not meet the requirements for the rapid and accurate identification of brucellosis; it delays the introduction of efficient remedial measures. On the other hand, these methods have limitations such as requiring complicated sample pretreatment, being time consuming and needing highly qualified personnel. To date, some of immunosensors for detection of Brucella were also reported [2,3]. For example, a novel amperometric immunosensor was prepared by Li and coworkers [3] which uses horseradish peroxidase (HRP) as a label in conjunction with a current-based Brucella sensor. In this work, the bacteria modified immunosensor was constructed by using a bio-composite formed by dispersing graphite powder into a mixture of Brucella melitensis and silicate polymer gel. The enzyme-labeled antibody can readily diffuse toward the encapsulated antigen (Brucella melitensis), which retains its binding properties, and the association reaction is easily detected at the surface exposed to the solution. In this report, a relatively low detection limit of 3.5 ng/ml and a linear detection range of 3.5 ng/ml to 200 ng/ml was obtained. Also, Liebes and colleagues have described the ideal conditions for the chemical modification of the optical fibers using Si-Bz and binding Brucella cells as receptors onto the fibers. Using such a method the immunosensor detected anti-Brucella IgG antibodies at low concentration of 207 ng/ml [4]. Also, these researchers proposed a novel optical method on the application of chemiluminescence OFIS in detection of Brucella cells exhibiting an A-antigen structure of their O-LPS. Compared to conventional colorimetric and chemiluminescence, the immunosensor detected a lower number of Brucella cells, about 305 cfu/ml and the reaction was exclusively specific to Brucella A O-LPS antigen. This establishes a novel approach for specifically detecting Brucella organisms in a point of care real time situation.

However, inspection of these methods based on immune-complexion show that all of these immunosensors need to label of biomolecules which is necessary for detection based on immune-reaction. Therefore, development of rapid, inexpensive, and easy-to-use methodologies for detection of Brucella has attracted considerable interest [2–4]. One of important method is DNA based biosensing [5–7].

In this work, a novel electrodeposition method for preparation of an excellent genosensor on the surface of low toxic substrate of gold nanoparticles supported by histidine, was fabricated. In summary, the gold nanoparticles were employed for immobilization of Brucella-specific probe in order to sensitively detect hybridization of a variety of sequences. The sensor modification was monitored by surface characterization and electrochemical techniques such as Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and square wave voltammetry (SWV). According to obtained results, the drop in the SWV peak current of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was attributed to the response of the DNA sequence binding to the sensor substrate. This biosensor has the potential to be used as a tool for Brucellosis diagnosis, by integrating it with other conventional tests. The high sensitivity would be related to more favorable conformation and deflection angle of the probe for an efficient hybridization, higher surface concentration of the probe, and/or enhanced diffusion regime. These lead to better display of the entangled target sequences arising from the nano-biotechnology.

All of oligunecluietide sequences including p-ssDNA sequence: 5′ SH‑TGC CGA TCA CTT AAG GGC CTT CAT 3′, t-ssDNA sequence: 5′‑ATG AAG GCC CTT AAG TGA TCG GCA‑3′, nc-ssDNA sequence: 5′‑AGA CCA AAA AGG CCA CCC CCG GGT‑3′, forward primer (5′‑GAC GAA CGA AAT TTT TCC AAT CCC‑3′), revers primer (5′‑TGC CGA TCA CTT AAG GGC CTT CAT 3′) were obtained from SinaClon BioScience Co. (Iran). The oligonucleotide stock solutions were diluted with 0.1 M Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.4 solution (Tris). Potassium ferrocyanide K4Fe(CN)6, potassium ferricyanide K3Fe(CN)6, disodium hydrogen orthophosphate, potassium dihydrogen orthophosphate potassium chloride, sodium nitrite, hydrochloric acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III)hydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Ontario, Canada). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution (0.1 M) was prepared by Na2HPO4 (0.1 M) and NaH2PO4 (0.1 M) with 0.15 M of NaCl in deionized water. Brucella agar culture medium with the addition of 5% horse blood was used for the cultivation of Brucella organisms by incubation at 35 ± 2 °C for 48–72 h. DNA samples for PCR amplification were extracted from Brucella strains, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, E. coli, Bacillus anthracis. The PCR reaction was performed on an Eppendorf Mastercycler Gradient PCR system (USA). To measure the concentration of the extracted DNA samples, Thermo Scientific NanoDrop 2000c (USA) was employed.

Electrochemical measurements were conducted using conventional three-electrode cell (from Metrohm), containing Ag/AgCl-saturated KCl as reference, a platinum wire as counter electrodes and gold (Au) working electrode (d = 2 mm) which powered by an electrochemical system comprising of Palm sens system with PS4.F1.05 (Palm instruments, Utrecht, The Netherlands). The system was run on a PC using PSTrance 5.3 software. The ac voltage amplitude used was 10 mV and the equilibrium time was 5 s. An Ag/AgCl-Sat'd KCl (from Metrohm) and a platinum wire were used as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was a gold (Au) electrode (from Azar Electrode Co., Urumia, Iran). For square-wave voltammetry (SWV) measurements, amplitude time = 0.02 s, stop potential = 0.001 V, frequency = 10 Hz and a scan rate of 0.05 V/s were employed. All the electroanalytical tests were carried out at 25 °C in 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. The surface morphology of electrode surface was characterized by high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi-SU8020, Czech) with an operating voltage of 3 kV, and chemical compositions of the electrode were analyzed by an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) coupled with the FE-SEM equipment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the Au electrode modified by gold nanoparticles supported by histidine (nan-Au-Hist)
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have revealed dependency of deposition potential on the size and morphology of generated gold nanoparticles (Au NPs). Shi et al. demonstrated that the crystal density of generated gold nanoparticles increases by shifting toward negative electrodeposition potentials from −0.3 to 0.3 V on ITO electrode, because of increase in nucleation rate when oversaturation occurs at higher overpotentials [8]. The reduction of AuCl4 − on Au electrode initiates at 0.15 V, leading to full nucleation at E = 0.20 V, therefore, the potential was adjusted on E = 0 V where no reaction occurs [9]. For electrodeposition of gold nanoparticles on the surface of Au electrode, the electrodeposition of metallic gold ions by chronoamperometry technique was carried out in the cell containing triple solution of 0.01 M HAuCl4, 0.1 M H2SO4 and 0.1 M Histidine. The electrodeposition potential was set at E = 0.0 V and time duration of the chronoamperometry was adjusted at 300 s (Fig. 1 ). For the fabrication mechanism of gold nanoparticles, it can be mentioned that gold atoms or clusters are initially and rapidly formed, followed by promoted nucleation [8,9]. The histidine adsorption process causes to formation of Au NPs-stable histidine layer in which histidine is involved to the surface via the zwitterions functional group, and its positively charged guanidinium functional group tends outward into the solution. Therefore, AuCl4 − ions was electrostatically absorb on the surface of gold electrode and cause to preferential growth of Au by the diffusion-limited aggregation mechanism.

As evident in Fig. 3 and Table 1 , signals for C and O elements belong to both Hist layers, as Hist include amine groups. The large and sharp Au peak is the typical signal for bulk gold element. The corresponding EDS spectra of Au/nano-Au-Hist demonstrates the increase in Au peak, which clearly indicates the electrodeposition of nano-size gold layer.

Affinity of amino acid to bind with gold surface has been investigated both theoretically [10,11] and experimentally [12,13]. Amino acids have also been employed as soft templates for the synthesis of gold nanostructures [14]. Gold has also affinity to amine functional group [15,16]. During the electrodeposition of gold, nucleation, adsorption (of additives), branching, and growth are the dominant steps. From these steps, kinetics of the growth step is slow and controls the total process [17,18]. At the early stage of the electrodeposition, AuCl4 − was quickly reduced to gold atoms, followed by distribution of gold nuclei at the surface. If an additive (amino acids in our case) is present in the solution, it is rapidly and selectively adsorbed (mainly via amine groups) on the specific planes of gold crystal. This adsorption prevents the newly generated gold atoms to be aggregated with the previously deposited ones. On the other hand, the additives act as shape directing agents and depending on their chemical structures facilitate the gold crystal growth at a specific direction(s). If the electrodeposition is performed at highly negative potentials, the hydrogen co-evolution process would be the main shape-directing agent.

Regarding the real surface areas, gold nanospears with small sawteeth had the highest and gold pine-like hyperbranched nanodendrites and gold nanoparticles had the lowest real surface areas. There was a wide range of roughness factor, and different shapes of gold provide huge different surface areas. Electrochemical activity of the gold structures was also different depending on the shape and size from both kinetic and thermodynamic points of view. The different peak currents for the electroreduction/electrooxidation of the analytes can be related to the active surface areas of the gold nanostructures and different shapes and sizes causing the reactions to occur at different potentials. It depended on both the entity of the analyte and the shape and size of the gold nanostructures; the best gold nanostructure can be selected for a special analyte to electroreduce/electrooxidize.

After electrodeposition of nano-Au-Hist layers on Au electrode, the stabilty of the layer was evaluated by CV and SWV techniques. As can be seen in Fig. 4 , this electrode is stable in three cycle. After that, the peak current was decreased siggnificantly.

Real surface areas of the synthesized gold nanostructures were electrochemically determined using the redox probe of ferrocyanide. Cyclic voltammograms of Fe(CN)6 4− recorded at different potential sweep rates using the gold-electrodeposited Au electrodes and the real surface area of the nanostructures were determined. Electrochemical activity of the synthesized gold nanostructures was evaluated in the course of electrooxidation/electroreduction.

Kinetic behavior of the proposed nano-layer was evaluated by performing CV in different scan rates from 0.02 to 1 V/s. As evident in the CV plot (Fig. 5A), the width of the voltammogram widened gradually and the three dominant peaks, one anodic and one cathodic peak raised considerably by increase in scan rate. It can be concluded that in the slower scan rates, the electron exchange occur slower at the electrode surface to record peak currents leading to narrower voltammogram with shorter current peaks.

The scan rate analysis revealed the direct dependence of the peak current on the selected range of square roots of sweep rates. In order to investigate the nature of the electrode reaction, dependency of the peak current versus the square root of the scan rate (v 1/2) and Napierian logarithm of peak current (Ln Ip) versus the Napierian logarithm of the sweep rate (ln v) for the peaks were plotted [19]. The linear dependency for Ip-v 1/2 and Ln Ip vs. Ln v, suggesting the diffusion controlled nature of the three electrochemical reactions (Fig. 5B & C). Therefore, it can be claimed that the electrochemical processes followed the Randles-Sevcik equation, specific for diffusion controlled electrochemical reactions [20]:
(1)
Ip = 2.69 ×
10 5
AD
1 / 2
n
3 / 2
v
1 / 2
C
where, Ip is the peak current (μA), A stands for the electroactive surface area (cm2), D attributes to the diffusion coefficient of the molecule in solution (cm2 s−1), n is the number of transferred electrons, v is the scan rate (V/s) and C corresponds to the bulk concentration of the analyte (mol·cm−3).

Also, the redox peak potential increased proportionally by the sweep rate indicating that sensor system has electro-catalytic property with irreversible nature. On the other hand, to elucidate the mechanism of mass transfer, two styles were used. I), Investigation on the variation of Ip versus v 1/2 (Fig. 5B). Using this approach, the intercept of the Ip versus v 1/2 was found to be 10−5, which indicates that the mass transfer was controlled by diffusion mechanism [18]. II) Investigation on the variation of ln Ip versus lnv. The results were illustrated in Fig. 5C. The slopes of the plot in Fig. 5C for oxidation peaks were found as 0.4523. If the slope of ln Ip versus ln v is close to 0.5, the process is diffusion controlled. Also, if the slope is close to 1, the process is controlled through adsorption [21]. As, the slope was calculated about 0.45, it can be concluded that the mass transfer is a diffusion controlled process which is in agreement with the results of the first approach.

In addition, the mass transfer mechanism can also be evaluated by electron transfer coefficient, α, of Eq. (2): [21].
(2)
Ep =
RT
2 αF
ln υ + constant
where E
p is electrode potential, R is gas constant (8.314, J K−1 mol−1), T is temperature in Kelvin scale (298 K), F is Faraday's constant (96,486, A·s·mol−1), and υ is potential sweep rate (V/s). If the value of α is close to 0.5, the system is a diffusion controlled [19]. Using this equation, the α value was obtained as 0.33 which further supports that the mass transfer of the system is diffusion controlled. In Fig. 5D, the plot of Eq. (2) obtained from ln Ip versus Ep (Tafel plot) have been illustrated.

The special surface coverage (Г
⁎ ) was calculated via the slope of the redox peak currents (A) versus sweep rate (V/s) using Eq. (3):
(3)
Ip =
n 2
F 2
4 RT
υA
Г ∗
where, A is total electrode surface (cm2), and Г
⁎ (mol cm−2) is special surface covered with Au/nano-Au-Hist. Based on the obtained results, the slope of redox peak currents versus sweep rates (Fig. 5E) was 0.0002 and the Г
⁎ value was calculated as 7.11 × 10−9 mol cm−2. Therefore, by using Au/nano-Au-Hist as electrode modifier, it is possible to increase the efficiency of the prepared genosensor. This improved efficiency could be a result of modifier and lead to also an increase in the conductivity.

Step by step preparation of genosensor was charecrized by SWV technique. Fig. 6 , show SWVs of nano-Au-Hist modified au electrode in various DNA sequence immobilization steps. Immobilization of p-ssDNA probe was performed by dropping 10.0 μl of 49.8 μM p-ssDNA solution dissolved in Tris on the nano-Au-Hist modified Au electrode and kept refrigerated at −4 °C for 4 h. Then the electrode was rinsed with Tris buffer. The DNA-modified Au/nano Au-His was further treated with 0.01 M 6‑Mercapto‑1‑hexanol at room temperature for 30 min to obtain a well aligned p-ssDNA monolayer. Then the electrode was washed again with Tris buffer and double distilled water, respectively, to remove unspecific absorbed p-ssDNA. The obtained electrode was denoted as the genosensor. The hybridization process was performed by immersing the genosensor into Tris containing various concentrations of t-ssDNA for 1 h at 37 °C. Then, the electrode was rinsed with Tris to remove the un-hybridized t-ssDNA, and then it was incubated in a solution containing Tris + NaCl (0.1 M) + methylene blue (MB, 20 μM) for 5 min at 37 °C. Finally, the electrode was rinsed with Tris to remove the physically absorbed MB. Fig. 6 shows SWVs of MB (as a common redox marker in genosensors [20]) interacted with p-ssDNA using genosensor, before and after hybridization of t-ssDNA recorded in 0.1 M Tris, pH 7.4 solution (Tris). MB can interact with both single stranded DNA (ssDNA) and dsDNA through different modes including electrostatic attractions and intercalation [21]. Under the ionic strength of the solution, MB binding to the free guanine bases in p-ssDNA is favorable [22] through hydrophobic forces of guanine-fused aromatic rings of MB. This fact is supported by a positive shift in the formal potential of MB on the genosensor surface [23].

A comparison was made between hybridization of t-ssDNA, and non-complementary sequence oligonucleotide (nc-ssDNA) with p-ssDNA. SWVs of MB interacting with the genosensor before and after hybridization with these sequences recorded in Tris-HCl are shown in Fig. 7 which indicate that nc-ssDNA has the minor affinity to hybridize with p-ssDNA. Therefore, the genosensor has excellent selectivity and distinguishing ability for the hybridization detection of mismatched target sequences.

For investigation the regeneration of the prepared DNA based biosensor, after its hybridization with 39.9 μM t-ssDNA, it was immersed in hot water of 80 °C for 10 min to de-hybridize the formed dsDNA. Then, the genosensor was re-hybridized with the same t-ssDNA concentration. According to Fig. 8 , SWVs showed a ∆I ≈ 0 for the regeneration of the DNA based biosensor.

Fig. 9A shows SWVs of MB interacted with p-ssDNA using the genosensor after hybridization with different concentrations of t-ssDNA recorded in 0.1 M Tris buffer, pH 7.4 solution. The dependency of the MB peak current on the t-ssDNA concentration was shown in Fig. 9B. The plot is linear with a regression equation of y = −38.15 + 525.98 (R2 = 0.9947 in the range of 1.0 × 10−1 to 1.0 × 10−11 μM of t-ssDNA with a low limit of quantification (LLOQ) 1.0 × 10−11 μM).

The stability of the DNA probe at the platform surface also plays important roles for the application and development of the sensor. The stability of the genosensor on the modifier surface was evaluated by measuring its SWV response at the electrode in a solution of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− (0.01 M), containing KCl. The stability of the genosensor was investigated by recording the peak currents in SWVs for 39.9 μM t-ssDNA over one week, while the genosensor was stored in Tris in the refrigerator at 4 °C and measured twice in a day in the same conditions which little change was observed at least for one week (Fig. 10 ). The current response (ΔI) of 500 μM gene after 7 days decreased by 100 μA. This indicates a defect in the electrode performance in different days of evaluation. Therefore, we suggest this sensor should be used as disposable sensor in clinical studies.

SWVs of the MB recorded using the genosensor at of bacterial genomes of Mycobacterium tuberculosis, E. coli, Bacillus anthracis were used to inspect the selectivity of prepared genosensor. In this part, these samples were analyzed with the genosensor. SWVs recorded for the non-Brucella genomic samples showed slight decrements in the peak currents for both concentrations, indicating selectivity of the genosensor toward Brucella species (Fig. 11 ).

Repeatability and reproducibility of the sensor play an important role in sensor performance. So, the necessary tests were done. The reproducibility of genosensor was investigated by detecting 500 pM of gene with three genosensor which were fabricated with the same procedure. The SWV response of three electrodes in a solution of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− (0.01 M), containing KCl, was recorded and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 5.11% was obtained, which showed the good reproducibility for the proposed genosensor.

For checking the repeatability of fabricated genosensor, the 500 pM of sequence were examined for three times under the same conditions in a solution of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− (0.01 M), containing KCl. The RSD of respond current was 4.11% which showed that the proposed genosensor had good repeatability.

For this purpose, DNA was extracted from one healthy and two patient human serum samples, amplified by PCR, de-hybridized, and then analyzed using the engineered genosensor. SWVs obtained using the genosensor for the healthy and patient samples revealed that the peak current related to the healthy sample was almost the same as p-ssDNA, while other samples represent drastic decrements. Therefore, the genosensor can detect the pathogen in patient after PCR amplification, and can be used in clinical diagnosis (Fig. 12 ).

In summary, a novel DNA-based platform consist of nano-Au-Hist was reported. The gold nanoparticles supported by histidine was checked for immobilization of a Brucella-specific probe and detection of hybridization with a variety of sequences with a high sensitivity. The high sensitivity would be related to more favorable conformation and deflection angle of the probe for an efficient hybridization, higher surface concentration of the probe, and/or enhanced diffusion regime. These lead to better display of the target sequences arising from the nano-biotechnology. The proposed genosensor showed a perfect discriminatory power between complementary, non-complementary and mismatched DNA sequences. The engineered genosensor for detection of the complementary/non-complementary sequences were assayed. The fabricated genosensor was evaluated for the assay of the bacteria in the cultured and human samples without polymerase chain reactions (PCR). The genosensor could detect the complementary sequence in linear concentration range of 1 × 10−1 to 1 × 10−10 μM, and a low limit of quantification 0.1 pM. Nano-Au combined with Hist enhances the performance of genosensor in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, wide dynamic range, low detection limit, good stability and impressive reusability incorporated with the capability to discriminate mismatched DNA from complementary target. These advantages could make this platform as a new paradigm in biosensor design.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Tabriz University of Medical Sciences.
